task: Do you recall where we left off on this commit? OUr session was interupted
yes please

Alright, we still have a lot of work to do on this branch. Please carefully review this feedback from the reviewer team and research the current state of the branch to determine a professional, enterprise-grade, determinsitic approach to making this a superb developer experience. The Azure DevOps SDK migration is mostly sound, but the artifact API-version cache can break Server compatibility on mixed-version endpoints, and the release
  workflow now depends on an unnecessary Playwright browser download. Either issue can cause real failures in supported environments.

  Full review comments:

  - [P2] Don't cache one build API version across all REST endpoints — E:\projects\ado-git-repo-insights\extension\ui\artifact-client.ts:247-250
    ArtifactClient now remembers the first working api-version and reuses it for every later call. The same client instance is used for getDefinitions(),
    getBuilds(), getArtifacts(), and file fetches, so on Azure DevOps Server deployments where those routes do not all support the same version, the first
    successful call locks the client to that version and later endpoints never get their fallback probe. In that environment, discovery/download starts failing
    with 400/404 even though an older version would have worked. Consider retrying on cached-version 400/404 or caching per endpoint family instead of globally.
  - [P2] Avoid extension postinstall in the release packaging job — E:\projects\ado-git-repo-insights.github\workflows\release.yml:104-106
    This new pnpm install --frozen-lockfile step runs extension/package.json's postinstall, which downloads Chromium via Playwright. That makes every release
    depend on an unrelated browser download before VSIX creation, so transient Playwright CDN failures or restricted runners will now block publishing even
    though packaging only needs the Node dependencies and tfx. Using --ignore-scripts (or another install path that skips Playwright) would keep release
    packaging determ

task: proceed
Excellent work. That fixed it. Do you notice anythinig we need to clean up to harden this?

Excellent! Please plan all four carefully. Let's not be short-sighted. We have a very strict review process that enforces strict typing, no any types, no lint warnings, no supressions, and a high degree of excellence, consistency, and professionalism.

Alright, our next challenge will require some deep exploration. I need your help understanding our publishing of the extension process. But it goes very very deep and we must resolve it comprehensively here and now. There are two major steps. 1. pnpm run package:vsix:dev current uses a special manfiest override. I need you to find all the files related to this and list them for me. 2. This repo has perfect version parity that is driven by semantic-release. This version parity impacts git, pipy, and ado marketplace extension versioning. Everything is currently dialed into the current published version (5.33.2). However, I manually published a version 99.0.x of our extension to the ado marketplace. That means we need to set a new baseline of 100.0.0, but we're not going to do that until we resolve problem 1. Got it?

Got it?

task:
Ok, excellent read. Now I need you to investigate one more item before we proceed with item 1. This review found a potential issue here that might be further corrupting our process. Please investigate this to see where it might fit into the picture. The new dev VSIX packaging script has a concrete argument-format bug that prevents its version override from being applied correctly. That makes the patch
  functionally incorrect for the newly introduced packaging workflow.

  Review comment:

  - [P2] Pass the dev version override JSON object, not a JSON string — E:\projects\ado-git-repo-insights\extension\scripts\package-dev-vsix.mjs:18-26
    Running pnpm run package:vsix:dev currently double-encodes the --override payload: override is already JSON.stringify({ version }), and line 26 stringifies
    it again before passing it to tfx. That sends a JSON string literal instead of the object tfx expects, so the dynamic version override is ignored or
    rejected. In practice this breaks the new dev-packaging flow because repeated uploads keep the manifest’s original version and get rejected as duplicates.

task: yes, commit

Ok, that should cover item 1 well. Now item 2. We have to be extra careful that our dev extension building process that we did (other than version 99) did not mess up our production semantic release process. It is critical that the production extension build and release process continues to work as it always has prior to this giant branch we are working on. Verify this completely before we move on to planning a fix. We have a very complex versioning strategy that span git release tags, pipy, azure devops marketplace extension, and the marketplace extension pipeline (like completely isolated). Everything is triggered automatically by semantic-release based on commitlinting by the release github actions after a successful merge to main. Once you fully understand this setup and if it has become corrupted at all beyond the published version 99 in ado marketplace, we must strategically set our new version baseline to 100 so then next release publishes 100 for everything.
user_feedback_text: Ok, that should cover item 1 well. Now item 2. We have to be extra careful that our dev extension building process that we did (other than version 99) did not mess up our production semantic release process. It is critical that the production extension build and release process continues to work as it always has prior to this giant branch we are working on. Verify this completely before we move on to planning a fix. We have a very complex versioning strategy that span git release tags, pipy, azure devops marketplace extension, and the marketplace extension pipeline (like completely isolated). Everything is triggered automatically by semantic-release based on commitlinting by the release github actions after a successful merge to main. Once you fully understand this setup and if it has become corrupted at all beyond the published version 99 in ado marketplace, we must strategically set our new version baseline to 100 so then next release publishes 100 for everything.

task: commit
excellent that worked well. Now, we have a completely new mission and this one won't be easy to prepare to investigation and research.

We current have codecov setup in CI as a code coverage gate. It works really awesome and I'm happy with it. However, we never catch the problems it catches in CI locally. Yet, we do have local coverage thresholds. Somehow a drift occurred that has become severe enough to make CI unpredictable. I understand that we'll likely always have some local/CI drift because codecov only runs in CI, but we also have a rather complex coverage mechanism of some sort that guards locally, right? And our README.md badges (python and ts) automatically update in CI. So all this is connected and it would seem we probably just need a minor modification locally to dial this in, no? For python and ts?
